Parallel Computing Practical

library(MASS)
library(doParallel)
library(foreach)

Question 1

q1 <- foreach(i = 1:100, .combine = rbind) %do% {
  samp <- rexp(100, 1)
  x_bar <- mean(samp)
  var_x <- var(samp)
  output <- c(mean = x_bar, variance = var_x)
}

q1
                mean  variance
result.1   0.9661875 0.9823502
result.2   0.9003252 0.6655827
result.3   0.9563530 0.9602820
result.4   1.0245658 1.0124356
result.5   1.0715958 1.0659552
result.6   0.9878099 0.8696043
result.7   0.9604665 0.7297878
result.8   0.9638504 0.6788426
result.9   0.9528731 0.8014086
result.10  1.0239484 0.6982384
result.11  1.1085422 1.2800335
result.12  0.8704886 0.4701909
result.13  1.2443681 1.1741737
result.14  1.0183081 0.8967335
result.15  0.9573172 1.0207439
result.16  0.9754892 1.0586474

result.19  1.0121910 0.9983437

result.59  1.1053451 0.9592157
result.60  1.0845507 1.2357418
result.61  0.9396363 1.1668152
result.62  1.2491530 1.9384649

result.67  1.0064015 0.9235809
result.68  1.1378164 1.3471054
result.69  1.0142568 1.5492332

result.75  0.9467124 0.9770227

result.77  1.1509138 1.4230848

result.82  0.9158850 0.7679653

result.98  0.9882714 1.1012862
result.99  1.0181537 1.0281946
result.100 0.9086645 0.7514528

Question 2

Parallel processing

Single bootstrap sample

library(doParallel)

gal <- galaxies
n <- length(gal)

c1 <- makeCluster(2)
registerDoParallel(c1)

system.time( {
  result_parallel <- foreach(i = 1:1000, .combine = c) %dopar% {
  samp <- sample(gal, n, replace = TRUE)
  med <- median(samp)
  }
})
   user  system elapsed 
   0.19    0.02    0.33 
stopCluster(c1)

1000 bootstrap samples

c2 <- makeCluster(2)
registerDoParallel(c2)

system.time( {
  results_large_p <- foreach(i = 1:100, .combine = c) %dopar% {
  vec <- numeric(1000)
  
  for (j in 1:1000) {
    s <- sample(gal, n, replace = TRUE)
    vec[j] <- median(s)
  }
  
  }
})
   user  system elapsed 
   0.03    0.02    2.47 
stopCluster(c2)

Serial processing

Single bootstrap sample

system.time( {
  result_serial <- foreach(i = 1:1000, .combine = c) %do% {
    boot_samp <- sample(gal, n, replace = TRUE)
    med_samp <- median(boot_samp)
  }
})
   user  system elapsed 
   0.27    0.00    0.27

1000 bootstrap samples

system.time( {
  results_large_s <- foreach(i = 1:100, .combine = c) %do% {
  vec <- numeric(1000)
  
  for (j in 1:1000) {
    s <- sample(gal, n, replace = TRUE)
    vec[j] <- median(s)
  }
  
  }
})
   user  system elapsed 
   4.47    0.11    4.60

From the results above, we see that the total run time for parallel processing is higher than for serial processing. However, with larger chunks we can see that using a parallel approach significantly decreases the processing time, thereby improving efficiency compared to serial processing.

Question 3

c3 <- makeCluster(4)
registerDoParallel(c3)

boot <- foreach(i = 1:10, .combine = rbind) %dopar% {

  exp_samp <- rexp(50, 1)
  boot_means <- numeric(100)
    
  for (j in 1:100) {
    b_sample <- sample(exp_samp, 50, replace = TRUE)
    boot_means[j] <- mean(b_sample) 
  }
  
  sorted <- sort(boot_means)
  lower <- sorted[ceiling(0.025*100)]
  upper <- sorted[ceiling(0.975*100)]
    
  boot_int <- quantile(boot_means, c(0.025, 0.975))
  
as.numeric(lower <= 1 && upper >= 1)

}

(coverage <- mean(boot))
[1] 1
stopCluster(c3)

Question 4

library(iterators)

set.seed(1234)

iter1 <- irnorm(5)
iter2 <- irnorm(5)
iter3 <- irnorm(5)

c4 <- makeCluster(3)
registerDoParallel(c4)

result <- foreach(i = iter1, j = iter2, k = iter3, .combine = rbind) %dopar% { 
  c("vector 1" = i, "vector 2" = j, "vector 3" = k) 
}

 max(result[ ,1])
 max(result[ ,2])
 max(result[ ,3])
stopCluster(c4)

Question 5

library(parallel)
library(foreach)
library(doParallel)

set.seed(1234)
v1 <- rnorm(5)
v2 <- rnorm(5)
v3 <- rnorm(5)

vectors <- list(v1, v2, v3)

c5 <- makeCluster(3)
registerDoParallel(c5)

Time for parLapply

system.time({
  res_par <- parLapply(c5, vectors, max)
})
   user  system elapsed 
      0       0       0

Time for replicate

system.time({
  result_rep <- replicate(3, max(rnorm(5)))
})
   user  system elapsed 
      0       0       0

Time for foreach

system.time({
  res_foreach <- foreach(v = vectors) %dopar% {
    max(v)
  }
})
   user  system elapsed 
   0.00    0.00    0.03 
stopCluster(c5)

From the above results, we see that the replicate function is the most efficient, with the smallest elapsed time. It is a sequential function, which usually works better for smaller data. The least efficient is parLapply, which is slightly slower than the foreach function.
